points.text: COVID-19 is mainly transmitted through inhaling air contaminated by droplets and aerosols containing the virus.
Infected individuals exhale particles when they breathe, talk, cough, sneeze, or sing.
Transmission is most likely at close range but can occur over longer distances, especially indoors.
COVID-19 can also spread when infectious particles come into contact with the eyes, nose, or mouth.
Small airborne particles can remain suspended in the air and travel longer distances indoors.
Touching surfaces contaminated with the virus and then touching the face can also lead to transmission.
People can spread the virus even if they do not develop symptoms, remaining contagious for up to 20 days.
COVID-19 spreads mainly from person to person through respiratory droplets.
The virus can be transmitted when an infected person coughs, sneezes, or talks.
Droplets from an infected person's breath can land on nearby individuals.
COVID-19 can spread in areas with low airflow, increasing transmission risk.
Close contact with an infected person increases the risk of transmission.
Spending time in crowded spaces with poor airflow raises the risk of catching COVID-19.
It's possible to contract COVID-19 from someone who shows no symptoms.
The virus can mutate, potentially leading to reinfection despite previous immunity.
The virus can spread from an infected person's mouth or nose in small droplets when they cough, sneeze, or breathe.
Particles can range in size from larger respiratory droplets to smaller aerosols.
People can be contagious whether or not they are displaying symptoms.
Infection can also occur by touching the mouth, nose, or eyes after touching contaminated surfaces.
